input <- input %>% dplyr::select(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens, contains("sex"), contains("cov_cat_age"))[...]levels(input$cov_cat_age_group) <- c("18-39", "40-59", "60-79", "80+")[...]input_sex_m <- input %>% filter(cov_cat_sex == "M")[...]input_sex_f <- input %>% filter(cov_cat_sex == "F")[...]dat_age <- round_km(input_sex, "lcovid_surv", "lcovid_cens", "cov_cat_age_group", threshold=6)[...]ggplot(dat_age, 
       aes(x_date,y_prob, group = cov_cat_age_group, color=cov_cat_age_group, 
           linetype = cov_cat_age_group)) +[...]facet_grid(cols = vars(sex))

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the primary cohort estimating hazard ratios for demographic predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Demographic predictors: “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” Detail on categories: “Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.” “Obesity was grouped based on body mass index (BMI kg/m2) …: no evidence of obesity BMI<30; obese class I, BMI 30-34.9; obese class II, BMI 35-39.9; and obese class III, BMI ≥ 40.” “Ethnic groups were White, Mixed, Asian or Asian British, Black or Black British and Chinese or other ethnic groups.” “IMD was determined … categorised into five quintiles… with quintile 1 (Q1) being the most deprived, and quintile 5 (Q5) being the least deprived.” Cohort: “a primary general population cohort, with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data handling: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Demographic factors – Primary and post-COVID diagnosis cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: The specific type of statistical test used for this analysis (e.g., logistic regression, Hazard Ratio).

                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)

result.code_value:
surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))]
if(analysis != "infected"){
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}else{
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"] # remove age
cov_names <- c(cov_factor_names, cov_num_names)
for(i in 1:length(cov_names)){
  surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
  fit_cox_model <- fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
  which_model = cov_names[i]
  output_file = paste0("output/review/model/HR_age_sex_adjusted_", which_model, "_", analysis)
  results <- cox_output2(fit_cox_model, which_model, output_file, save_output=FALSE)
}
[...]input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
result.explanation: The paper specifies an age- and sex-adjusted Cox model for time to first coded long COVID and lists demographic predictors, with age modeled via spline. The code implements Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) with cov_cat_sex and rcs(cov_num_age) and adds each covariate in turn, matching the paper’s variable roles.

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the primary cohort estimating hazard ratios for demographic predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Demographic predictors: “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” Detail on categories: “Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.” “Obesity was grouped based on body mass index (BMI kg/m2) …: no evidence of obesity BMI<30; obese class I, BMI 30-34.9; obese class II, BMI 35-39.9; and obese class III, BMI ≥ 40.” “Ethnic groups were White, Mixed, Asian or Asian British, Black or Black British and Chinese or other ethnic groups.” “IMD was determined … categorised into five quintiles… with quintile 1 (Q1) being the most deprived, and quintile 5 (Q5) being the least deprived.” Cohort: “a primary general population cohort, with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data handling: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Demographic factors – Primary and post-COVID diagnosis cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…”[...]“For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.”[...]“We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
result.code_value:
knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))[...]surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)[...]ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information[...]non_case_inverse_weight=(nrow(input)-nrow(cases))/nrow(non_cases)
input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                                    non_case_inverse_weight, 1)[...]robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id)[...]levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"[...]mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = replace_na(cov_cat_smoking_status, "Missing"))
result.explanation: The implementation models age with restricted cubic splines, applies 1:20 non-case sampling with inverse-probability weights, and uses robust SEs; missing categories are created for IMD and smoking status as specified. These correspond to the paper’s parameter specifications for the age- and sex-adjusted Cox models.

We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” [...] “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”

result.paper_value: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”[...]“Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.”[...]“Hazard ratios by age group (40 to 59, 60 to 79 and 80 to 105 years compared with 18 to 39 years(reference)), were estimated from models including age as a categorical variable, instead of a cubic spline.”
result.code_value:
levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
input$cov_cat_smoking_status <- ordered(input$cov_cat_smoking_status, levels = c("Never smoker","Ever smoker","Current smoker","Missing"))
input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity, ref = "White")
input$cov_cat_sex <- relevel(input$cov_cat_sex, ref = "M")
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ""
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=18 & input$cov_num_age<=39, "18_39", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=40 & input$cov_num_age<=59, "40_59", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=60 & input$cov_num_age<=79, "60_79", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=80, "80_105", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- factor(input$cov_cat_age_group, ordered = TRUE)
input_factor_vars <- input_factor_vars %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.character(cov_cat_smoking_status)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = replace_na(cov_cat_smoking_status, "Missing")) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.factor(cov_cat_smoking_status))
result.explanation: The paper specifies missing categories for ethnicity, smoking, and IMD and defines age groups with 18–39 as the reference; the code implements these by creating explicit “Missing” levels, ordering IMD with a “0 (missing)” level, and coding age groups 18_39/40_59/60_79/80_105 as an ordered factor.

evidence.paper_span: Methods: Statistical analyses — “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”; Patient characteristics — “Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.”; Statistical analyses — “Hazard ratios by age group (40 to 59, 60 to 79 and 80 to 105 years compared with 18 to 39 years(reference)), were estimated from models including age as a categorical variable, instead of a cubic spline.”

result.paper_value: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID 6 in English primary care records, consisting of two diagnostic codes, three referral codes and 10 assessment codes (Supplement, Table S2). Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date (Supplement, Table S1).” [...] “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” [...] “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors that may be associated with coded long COVID 6 7 , and the frequency of GP-patient interactions, which could be an indicator of patient access to care and ability to interact with GP. [...] History of the disease diagnoses, chosen based on previous literature on risk factors for long COVID 7 and defined on or before the cohort and participant specific follow-up start date, was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” [...] “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”
result.code_value:
covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]

covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]

surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...] 
fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula),
                                 data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies the outcome and that all patient characteristics (demographics, behaviours, and listed comorbidities) are included in a multivariable Cox model. The code builds a Cox model with Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) and includes all covariates prefixed with “cov_” (i.e., all patient characteristics) plus age as a spline.

evidence.paper_span: Methods: Statistical analyses — “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model. Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline, and estimated log hazard ratios against continuous age were plotted. In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor. For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.”

input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),
                                          "non_hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date) & 
                                            !is.na(input$hospital_covid) &
                                            (input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date>=0 &
                                               input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date<29),
                                          "hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
[...]
if(analysis == "infected"){
    surv_formula <- paste0(surv_formula, " + cov_cat_covid_phenotype")
}
result.explanation: The paper specifies COVID-19 severity coded as hospitalised vs non-hospitalised in the post-COVID cohort, and the code defines these categories and includes the variable in the infected cohort model. This matches the paper’s coding specification.

result.code_value:
covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]
covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
[...]surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...]if(cohort == "infected"){
    input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_covid_phenotype = sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
}
result.explanation: The code defines time-to-event for first coded long COVID and fits a multivariable Cox model including all covariates (with age as a spline), and for the post-COVID (infected) cohort it adds the COVID phenotype (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised). This matches the paper’s specified variables and designations.

input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- "no_infection"
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),
                                          "non_hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date) & 
                                          !is.na(input$hospital_covid) &
                                          (input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date>=0 &
                                             input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date<29),
                                        "hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
[...]

    input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_covid_phenotype = sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
  }

Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date (Supplement, Table S1).[...]Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.[...]a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022.
result.code_value:
if(analysis == "all_vax_c"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_all.rds")
  input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-lcovid_surv, -lcovid_cens) %>%
    dplyr::rename(lcovid_surv = lcovid_surv_vax_c, lcovid_cens = lcovid_cens_vax_c)
}
[...]surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))]
if(analysis != "infected"){
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}else{
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"] # remove age
[...]surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
fit_cox_model <- fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
[...]input_select <- input_select %>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date_vax_c=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                                 vax_covid_date1,
                                                                                 death_date, 
                                                                                 cohort_end_date,
                                                                                 na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv_vax_c<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date_vax_c - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens_vax_c = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date_vax_c & 
                                                                          out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                          !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
result.explanation: The paper specifies age- and sex-adjusted Cox models estimating hazard ratios for each characteristic with long COVID as the outcome and pre-vaccination censoring. The code fits Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) models with cov_cat_sex and spline age plus each covariate, and for the pre-vaccination cohort uses vaccination-censored survival times.

analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. result.paper_value: “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022” [...] “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” [...] “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” [...] “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors that may be associated with coded long COVID… Smoking status… frequency of GP-patient interaction… history of disease diagnoses… asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” [...] “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”
result.code_value:
if(analysis == "all_vax_c"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_all.rds")
  input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-lcovid_surv, -lcovid_cens) %>%
    dplyr::rename(lcovid_surv = lcovid_surv_vax_c, lcovid_cens = lcovid_cens_vax_c)
}
[...]
input_select <- input_select %>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date_vax_c=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                                 vax_covid_date1,
                                                                                 death_date, 
                                                                                 cohort_end_date,
                                                                                 na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv_vax_c<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date_vax_c - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens_vax_c = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date_vax_c & 
                                                                          out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                          !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...]
covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]
## remove categorical and continuous age, as continuous age will be added
## later as with a spline function
covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
[...]
surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]
fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula),
                                 data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies a multivariable Cox model in the pre-vaccination cohort using all patient characteristics, with outcome time to first coded long COVID. The code censors at first vaccination, uses Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) and includes all covariates with prefix “cov_” (minus age, which is added as a spline), matching the paper.

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the post-vaccination cohort estimating hazard ratios for demographic predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Demographics: “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” Post-vaccination cohort: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”

result.code_value:
input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 [...] input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0)) [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))]
cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")]
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"]
surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
result.explanation: The paper specifies an age- and sex-adjusted Cox model for time to first coded long COVID in the post-vaccination cohort with demographic predictors; the code sets post-vaccination follow-up from 14 days after dose 2, defines the survival outcome, and fits Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) models including cov_cat_sex and cov_num_age with each predictor. This aligns with the described variable roles (outcome, time scale, adjusters, and demographic predictors).

result.paper_value: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022.”[...]“In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”[...]“Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…”[...]“For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. result.code_value:
if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
    input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
}
[...]
if(analysis == "vaccinated"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_vaccinated.rds")
}
[...]
ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information
[...]
input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                                    non_case_inverse_weight, 1)
[...]
knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))
[...]
surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]
fit_cox_model <- fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
[...]
robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id)
results$robust.conf.low=exp(confint(robust_fit_cox_model,level=0.95)[,1])
result.explanation: The code sets post-vaccination follow-up to 14 days after the second dose, models age with restricted cubic splines, uses 1:20 sampling with inverse probability weighting, and computes robust SEs, matching the paper’s parameter specifications for the age- and sex-adjusted model.

We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.”

if(analysis == "vaccinated"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_vaccinated.rds")
} [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i]) [...] input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)")) [...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing")) [...] input_factor_vars$cov_cat_smoking_status <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_smoking_status, ref = "Never smoker") [...] input$cov_cat_sex <- relevel(input$cov_cat_sex, ref = "M") [...] input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=18 & input$cov_num_age<=39, "18_39", input$cov_cat_age_group)
  input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=40 & input$cov_num_age<=59, "40_59", input$cov_cat_age_group)
  input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=60 & input$cov_num_age<=79, "60_79", input$cov_cat_age_group)
  input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=80, "80_105", input$cov_cat_age_group)
  input$cov_cat_age_group <- factor(input$cov_cat_age_group, ordered = TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies restricted cubic splines for age, explicit age-group categories, and inclusion of missing categories for covariates; the code implements age via rcs(), defines IMD with a '0 (missing)' level, recodes smoking to include 'Missing', sets reference levels, and constructs age- and sex-adjusted models for the vaccinated cohort.

result.code_value:
if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
  input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
  input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
}
[...]input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...]if(analysis == "vaccinated"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_vaccinated.rds")
}
[...]covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]

covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]

surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The code defines the post-vaccination cohort’s start at 14 days after second dose, constructs time-to-first long COVID event, and fits a multivariable Cox model including all covariates (with age as a spline), matching the paper’s specification. The included predictors correspond to the paper’s demographic and clinical variables under “all patient characteristics.”

non_case_inverse_weight=(nrow(input)-nrow(cases))/nrow(non_cases)

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the primary cohort estimating hazard ratios for health behavioural and clinical predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical predictors: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking status: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP-patient interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease history list: “…separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke. History of diagnosed post-viral fatigue was defined prior to 29 January 2020 …” Cohort: “a primary general population cohort, with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Primary and post-COVID diagnosis cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: The specific type of statistical test used for this analysis (e.g., logistic regression, Hazard Ratio).

)
[...]

  surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
  [...] 
  fit_cox_model <- fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
}

  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",

}else{
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"] # remove age
cov_names <- c(cov_factor_names, cov_num_names)
surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
fit_cox_model <- fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the primary cohort estimating hazard ratios for health behavioural and clinical predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical predictors: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking status: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP-patient interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease history list: “…separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke. History of diagnosed post-viral fatigue was defined prior to 29 January 2020 …” Cohort: “a primary general population cohort, with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Primary and post-COVID diagnosis cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any parameters which are set for the analysis (e.g., assumptions of equal groups).

result.paper_value: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline, and estimated log hazard ratios against continuous age were plotted.” [...] “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.”
result.code_value:
ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information [...] non_case_inverse_weight=(nrow(input)-nrow(cases))/nrow(non_cases)
input$weight <-1
input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                                    non_case_inverse_weight, 1) [...] knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9))) [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id)
result.explanation: The paper specifies restricted cubic splines for age, a 1:20 sampling ratio with inverse probability weighting, and robust standard errors; the code implements rcs for age, sets ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 with IPW weights, and uses rms::robcov for robust SEs. These parameter settings align.

result.paper_value: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID … Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date …” [...] “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors that may be associated with coded long COVID … and the frequency of GP-patient interactions … Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” [...] “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” [...] “History of the disease diagnoses … was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” [...] “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model. Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline.”
result.code_value:
covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]
[...] covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
[...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...] fit_cox_model_splines <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula),
                                 data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies time to first coded long COVID and includes all listed patient characteristics as predictors in a multivariable Cox model with age as a restricted cubic spline. The code builds Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) and fits a Cox model with all covariates prefixed by “cov_” (excluding age, then adding rcs(age)), matching the paper’s variable specification.

evidence.paper_span: Outcomes: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID … Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date …” [...] Patient characteristics: “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors … Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” [...] “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” [...] “History of the disease diagnoses … was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” [...] Statistical analyses: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model. Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline.”

result.code_value:
ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information [...] non_case_inverse_weight=(nrow(input)-nrow(cases))/nrow(non_cases) [...] input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                       non_case_inverse_weight, 1) [...] knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9))) [...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id)
result.explanation: The code uses 1:20 sampling with inverse probability weighting, models age via restricted cubic splines, and computes robust standard errors, matching the paper’s parameter specifications.

result.paper_value: “Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.” [...] “History of the disease diagnoses, chosen based on previous literature on risk factors for long COVID 7 and defined on or before the cohort and participant specific follow-up start date, was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.”
result.code_value:
## remove ra-sle-psoriasis because they have been included individually
input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-cov_cat_ra_sle_psoriasis) %>%
    rename(cov_num_gp_patient_interaction = cov_num_gp_consultation)
[...] 
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ""
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=18 & input$cov_num_age<=39, "18_39", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=40 & input$cov_num_age<=59, "40_59", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=60 & input$cov_num_age<=79, "60_79", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=80, "80_105", input$cov_cat_age_group)
input$cov_cat_age_group <- factor(input$cov_cat_age_group, ordered = TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies age grouping and disease history as separate indicators; the code implements those same age group cut-points and treats RA/SLE/psoriasis as separate indicators rather than a combined variable. This matches the stated coding specifications.

result.paper_value: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” [...] “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” [...] “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID 6 in English primary care records, consisting of two diagnostic codes, three referral codes and 10 assessment codes (Supplement, Table S2). Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date (Supplement, Table S1).” [...] “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” [...] “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” [...] “Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” [...] “History of the disease diagnoses, chosen based on previous literature on risk factors for long COVID 7 and defined on or before the cohort and participant specific follow-up start date, was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.”
result.code_value:
source("analysis/stage2_model_input_set_up.R") [...] cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))] [...] if(analysis != "infected"){
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")]
}else{
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status")]
} [...] cov_names <- c(cov_factor_names, cov_num_names) [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i]) [...] fit_cox_model <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE) [...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing")) [...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
result.explanation: The code fits age- and sex-adjusted Cox models using Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) and adds each predictor (including smoking, GP-patient interaction, disease histories), while retaining COVID-19 severity in the infected cohort. This aligns with the paper’s specified outcome, adjusters, predictors, and inclusion of severity in the post-COVID diagnosis cohort.

analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking categories: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction… categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease history list: “…asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” Additional factor for this cohort: “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Primary and post-COVID diagnosis cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any pre-specified inference criteria used for the analysis (e.g., alpha = 0.05, confidence intervals excluding particular values).

result.paper_value: In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.
result.code_value:
results$robust.conf.low=exp(confint(robust_fit_cox_model,level=0.95)[,1]) #use robust standard errors to calculate 95% CI
results$robust.conf.high=exp(confint(robust_fit_cox_model,level=0.95)[,2])
result.explanation: The paper specifies 95% confidence intervals for hazard ratios, and the code computes 95% CIs (level=0.95), using robust standard errors when applicable. This matches the stated inference specification.

result.paper_value: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” [...] “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” [...] “Demographic variables included age, sex, obesity, ethnicity, region and deprivation.” [...] “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” [...] “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.”
result.code_value:
if(analysis == "infected"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_infected.rds")
}
[...] covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]

## remove categorical and continuous age, as continuous age will be added
## later as with a spline function
covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
[...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The paper specifies time to first coded long COVID as the outcome and inclusion of all patient characteristics plus COVID-19 severity in the post‑COVID cohort. The code fits Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) using all covariates prefixed “cov_” (which includes cov_cat_covid_phenotype for the infected cohort) with age modeled by a spline.

result.paper_value: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” [...] “Obesity was grouped based on body mass index (BMI kg/m 2 ) using categories derived from the World health organization (WHO) 13 : no evidence of obesity BMI<30; obese class I, BMI 30-34.9; obese class II, BMI 35-39.9; and obese class III, BMI ≥ 40.” [...] “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” [...] “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” [...] “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
result.code_value:
options(datadist="dd", contrasts=c("contr.treatment", "contr.treatment")) [...] knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))

surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
input$cov_cat_smoking_status <- ordered(input$cov_cat_smoking_status, levels = c("Never smoker","Ever smoker","Current smoker","Missing")) [...] input$cov_cat_bmi<- ordered(input$cov_cat_bmi, 
                              levels = c("Not obese","Obese I (30-34.9)",
                                          "Obese II (35-39.9)", "Obese III (40+)")) [...] levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)")) [...] input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- "no_infection"
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),
                                          "non_hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date) & 
                                            !is.na(input$hospital_covid) &
                                            (input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date>=0 &
                                               input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date<29),
                                          "hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- as.factor(input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype) [...] if(cohort == "infected"){
    input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_covid_phenotype = sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
} [...] input$cov_cat_sex <- relevel(input$cov_cat_sex, ref = "M")
input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity, ref = "White")
result.explanation: The paper specifies categorical coding (e.g., smoking, BMI), inclusion of a missing category for selected variables, an age spline, and adding COVID-19 severity in the post-COVID cohort; the code implements these via factor recoding/levels, treatment contrasts, restricted cubic splines for age, and a hospitalised vs non-hospitalised severity variable for the infected cohort. Reference levels (e.g., Male for sex, White for ethnicity) and missing category handling (IMD, smoking) are explicitly shown in code.

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the pre-vaccination cohort estimating hazard ratios for health behavioural and clinical predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking categories: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction… categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease list (indicators): “…asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” Pre-vacc cohort: “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking categories: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction… categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease list (indicators): “…asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” Pre-vacc cohort: “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. result.paper_value: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models, and then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”[...]“The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID 6 in English primary care records… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date (Supplement, Table S1).”[...]“a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022.”[...]“Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.”[...]“Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.”[...]“Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.”[...]“History of the disease diagnoses… coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.”
result.code_value:
surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]
if(analysis == "all_vax_c"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_all.rds")
  input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-lcovid_surv, -lcovid_cens) %>%
    dplyr::rename(lcovid_surv = lcovid_surv_vax_c, lcovid_cens = lcovid_cens_vax_c)
}
[...]
input_select <- input_select %>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date_vax_c=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                                 vax_covid_date1,
                                                                                 death_date, 
                                                                                 cohort_end_date,
                                                                                 na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv_vax_c<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date_vax_c - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens_vax_c = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date_vax_c & 
                                                                      out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                      !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...]
cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))]
if(analysis != "infected"){
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}else{
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"] # remove age
cov_names <- c(cov_factor_names, cov_num_names)
for(i in 1:length(cov_names)){
  surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
  fit_cox_model <- rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
}
[...]
input <- input%>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
[...]
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
result.explanation: The code implements an age- and sex-adjusted Cox model that, in the pre-vaccination ('all_vax_c') cohort, models time to first long COVID code with censoring at first vaccination and evaluates each health/clinical predictor in turn. It includes smoking status, GP-patient interaction categories, and other covariates (disease indicators) consistent with the paper’s variable specification.

knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))
surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...]ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information
...
non_case_inverse_weight=(nrow(input)-nrow(cases))/nrow(non_cases)
input$weight <-1
input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                                    non_case_inverse_weight, 1)
[...]robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id)
results$robust.conf.low=exp(confint(robust_fit_cox_model,level=0.95)[,1])

                                                                          out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                          !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the pre-vaccination cohort estimating hazard ratios for health behavioural and clinical predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking categories: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction… categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease list (indicators): “…asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” Pre-vacc cohort: “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Pre- and post-vaccination cohorts

comparisons.dimension_definition: Any specifications related to how variables are coded within the analyses (e.g., contrast coding with Intervention = 1 and Control = 0).

result.paper_value: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.”[...]“Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.”[...]“We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”[...]“Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…”
result.code_value:
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
input$cov_cat_smoking_status <- ordered(input$cov_cat_smoking_status, 
                               levels = c("Never smoker","Ever smoker","Current smoker","Missing"))
[...]
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
input <- input%>% rename(sub_num_gp_patient_interaction = cov_num_gp_patient_interaction) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = as.factor(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = relevel(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction, ref = "0"))
[...]
levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)"))
[...]
input_factor_vars <- input_factor_vars %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.character(cov_cat_smoking_status)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = replace_na(cov_cat_smoking_status, "Missing")) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.factor(cov_cat_smoking_status))
[...]
input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity, ref = "White")
[...]
knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))
surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The paper specifies smoking and GP-interaction categories, age as a spline, and a missing category for ethnicity, smoking, and IMD. The code implements the smoking and GP-interaction groupings, IMD/smoking missing categories, and age splines, but only relevels ethnicity to “White” without explicitly creating a “Missing” ethnicity category.

evidence.paper_span: Methods: Patient characteristics: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.”; “Frequency of GP-patient interaction…categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.”; Statistical analyses: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…”; “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
