result.paper_value: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” [...] “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors that may be associated with coded long COVID…, and the frequency of GP-patient interactions…” [...] “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” [...] “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022” [...] “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”
result.code_value:
if(analysis == "all_vax_c"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_all.rds")
  input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-lcovid_surv, -lcovid_cens) %>%
    dplyr::rename(lcovid_surv = lcovid_surv_vax_c, lcovid_cens = lcovid_cens_vax_c)
}
[...] input_select <- input_select %>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date_vax_c=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                                 vax_covid_date1,
                                                                                 death_date, 
                                                                                 cohort_end_date,
                                                                                 na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv_vax_c<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date_vax_c - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens_vax_c = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date_vax_c & 
                                                                          out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                          !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...] covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]
covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
[...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The paper defines the outcome/time scale and states that all patient characteristics are included, and the pre‑vaccination cohort ends at first vaccination. The code sets pre‑vaccination follow‑up, uses those times as Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens), and includes all covariates (all “cov_” variables, with age as a spline) in the multivariable Cox model.

evidence.paper_span: Outcomes: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…”; Patient characteristics: “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors… and the frequency of GP-patient interactions…”; “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.”; Cohorts: “a pre-vaccination cohort with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, date of receipt of first COVID-19 vaccine dose, death date, or 31 March 2022”; Statistical analyses: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”

input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,

result.explanation: The code censors follow-up at first vaccination for the pre-vaccination cohort, models age with restricted cubic splines (knots at the 10th/50th/90th percentiles), samples non-cases at 1:20 with inverse-probability weights, applies robust standard errors, and includes missing categories (e.g., IMD, smoking), matching the paper’s specifications.

surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
[...] input_factor_vars <- input_factor_vars %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.character(cov_cat_smoking_status)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = replace_na(cov_cat_smoking_status, "Missing")) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.factor(cov_cat_smoking_status))
[...] levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)"))
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
[...] input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity, ref = "White")
result.explanation: The code matches the paper on using restricted cubic splines for age and on how smoking, IMD, and GP interactions are coded, but it does not implement a ‘Missing’ category for ethnicity (it only sets White as the reference). Therefore, the coding specification does not fully align.

if(analysis == "vaccinated"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_vaccinated.rds")
}
[...] if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
    # select people who were vaccinated during the study period
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
    # reset index date to be 14 days following the second dose of vaccine
    input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
}
[...] input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
[...] cov_factor_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_cat", names(input))]
cov_num_names <- names(input)[grepl("cov_num", names(input))]
if(analysis != "infected"){
  cov_factor_names <- cov_factor_names[!cov_factor_names %in% c("cov_cat_sex","cov_cat_ie.status",
                                                              "cov_cat_covid_phenotype")] # remove sex and vaccination status
}
cov_num_names <- cov_num_names[! cov_num_names %in% "cov_num_age"] # remove age
cov_names <- c(cov_factor_names, cov_num_names)
[...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted <- paste0(surv_formula_age_spl_sex,"+", cov_names[i])
fit_cox_model <- rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_spl_sex_adjusted),
                                   data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
output_file = paste0("output/review/model/HR_age_sex_adjusted_", which_model, "_", analysis)
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
input <- input%>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
result.explanation: The paper specifies an age- and sex-adjusted Cox model in the post‑vaccination cohort with long COVID as the outcome and health behavioural/clinical predictors. The code implements Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) with index date 14 days after dose 2, fits age+sex (age spline) models adding each predictor, and includes smoking and GP‑patient interaction categories.

result.explanation: The code specifies the same parameters as the paper: age is modeled with restricted cubic splines in age–sex adjusted Cox models, post‑vaccination follow-up starts 14 days after dose 2, a 1:20 sampling with inverse-probability weights is used, robust SEs are computed, and missing categories are created. These align with the paper’s parameter specifications for the post‑vaccination cohort.

analysis.brief_description: Age- and sex-adjusted Cox proportional hazards model in the post-vaccination cohort estimating hazard ratios for health behavioural and clinical predictors of time to first coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “In each cohort, hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for each patient characteristic were estimated from age-and-sex adjusted Cox proportional hazards (PH) models…”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Smoking categories: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” GP interactions: “Frequency of GP-patient interaction… categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” Disease list (indicators): “…asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” Post-vacc cohort: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form (as adjuster): “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Pre- and post-vaccination cohorts

result.paper_value: “Smoking status was grouped into current-, ever-, and neversmokers.” [...] “Frequency of GP-patient interaction was defined during the 12 months prior to participants' follow-up start date, and categorised as: without any interaction; 1-3; 4-8; 9-12 and 13+ interactions.” [...] “History of the disease diagnoses, chosen based on previous literature on risk factors for long COVID 7 and defined on or before the cohort and participant specific follow-up start date, was coded as separate indicator variables: asthma, cancer, chronic cardiac disease, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic respiratory disease, dementia, diabetes, dysplenia (dysfunctional-spleen), haematological cancer, heart failure, hypertension, mental health condition, organ transplant, other immunosuppressive condition, other neurological condition, post-viral fatigue, psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, systematic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and stroke.” [...] “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.” [...] “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” [...] “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose”
result.code_value:
if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
    # select people who were vaccinated during the study period
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
    # reset index date to be 14 days following the second dose of vaccine
    input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
  }
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction > 12,
                                                             "13 or more",
                                                            ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >= 9,
                                                             "9 to 12",
                                                                ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=4,
                                                                      "4 to 8",
                                                                         ifelse(input$cov_num_gp_patient_interaction >=1,
                                                                                          "1 to 3", "0")))))
  [...] mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = as.factor(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction = relevel(cov_cat_gp_patient_interaction, ref = "0"))
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
  input$cov_cat_smoking_status <- ordered(input$cov_cat_smoking_status, levels = c("Never smoker","Ever smoker","Current smoker","Missing"))
[...] levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
[...] input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)"))
[...] knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))
[...] surv_formula_age_spl_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex",
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The code implements the same variable coding as the paper: smoking grouped into Never/Ever/Current with a Missing category, GP-patient interaction coded as 0, 1–3, 4–8, 9–12, 13+, IMD includes a missing category, and age is modeled using a restricted cubic spline; the post-vaccination cohort starts 14 days after second dose. These match the paper’s stated coding specifications for the age- and sex-adjusted models in the post-vaccination cohort.

result.code_value:
if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
    # select people who were vaccinated during the study period
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
    # reset index date to be 14 days following the second dose of vaccine
    input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
  }
[...]
input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
  input_select <- input_select %>% filter(fup_end_date >= index_date & fup_end_date != Inf)
  input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
  ## define event indicator, without censoring for vaccination
  input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...]
if(analysis == "vaccinated"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_vaccinated.rds")
}

covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]

covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]

surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The code sets the post‑vaccination follow-up to begin 14 days after the second dose, defines time to first coded long COVID from that start date, and fits a Cox model including all covariates (prefixed “cov_”) with age as a spline, matching the paper’s multivariable specification. The included predictors align with the paper’s demographic and clinical characteristics.

evidence.paper_span: Study population and cohort definitions: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022.” Methods – Outcomes: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Methods – Patient characteristics: “Patient characteristics included demographic variables, and health behavioural and clinical factors… Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Methods – Statistical analyses: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”

analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Scope of covariates: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” Health behavioural and clinical variables: “Health behavioural and clinical factors included smoking status, frequency of GP-patient interaction and history of disease diagnoses.” Post-vacc cohort: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of…”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”

result.paper_value: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline, and estimated log hazard ratios against continuous age were plotted.” [...] “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” [...] “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose”
result.code_value:
ratio_non_cases_to_cases = 20 # this is used in sampling non-cases to increase efficiency without loss of information [...] input$weight <- ifelse(input$patient_id %in% noncase_ids,
                                    non_case_inverse_weight, 1) [...] knot_placement=as.numeric(quantile(input$cov_num_age, probs=c(0.1,0.5,0.9)))

## Full model - age spline
surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
) [...] robust_fit_cox_model=rms::robcov(fit_cox_model, cluster = input$practice_id) [...] if(cohort == "vaccinated"){
    # select people who were vaccinated during the study period
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(vax_covid_date2) &vax_covid_date2 >= index_date & vax_covid_date2 <= cohort_end_date)
    # reset index date to be 14 days following the second dose of vaccine
    input$index_date = input$vax_covid_date2 + 14 
  }
result.explanation: The paper specifies RCS for age, a 1:20 non-case sampling with IPW and robust SEs, and post-vaccination follow-up starting 14 days after dose 2; the code implements these with rcs(), a 1:20 sampling ratio and weights plus robust covariance, and sets index_date = vax_covid_date2 + 14. These parameter choices align.

result.paper_value: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID, constructed from the date of the first record of any of the 15 UK SNOMED-CT codes for long COVID in English primary care records, consisting of two diagnostic codes, three referral codes and 10 assessment codes (Supplement, Table S2). Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date (Supplement, Table S1).” [...] “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline, and estimated log hazard ratios against continuous age were plotted.” [...] “a primary general population cohort, with follow-up start date 29 January 2020 and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022”
result.code_value:
index_date=as.Date("2020-01-29") [...] cohort_start = as.Date("2020-01-29", format="%Y-%m-%d") [...] input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0)) [...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The paper specifies coded long COVID as the outcome with time from follow-up start and models age using a restricted cubic spline in the primary cohort starting 29 Jan 2020. The code defines Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens), constructs time-to-event and event indicator from long COVID dates, sets the start date to 29 Jan 2020, and models age with rms::rcs(cov_num_age,...).

result.code_value:
if(cohort == "infected"){
    # select people who were infected during the study period
    input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(out_covid_date) &out_covid_date>= index_date & out_covid_date <= cohort_end_date)
    # reset index date to be the date of covid infection
    input$index_date = input$out_covid_date
}
[...] input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...] if(cohort == "infected"){
    input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_covid_phenotype = sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
}
[...] if(analysis == "infected"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_infected.rds")
}
[...] surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The paper specifies the post-COVID cohort’s outcome, time origin, inclusion of COVID-19 severity, and age as a restricted cubic spline, and the code implements exactly this cohort with index_date at first COVID diagnosis, outcome as lcovid_surv/lcovid_cens, includes cov_cat_covid_phenotype, and models age via rms::rcs. Thus, the variable specification aligns.

result.code_value:
levels(input$cov_cat_imd)[levels(input$cov_cat_imd)==0] <-"0 (missing)"
input$cov_cat_imd <- ordered(input$cov_cat_imd, 
                               levels = c("1 (most deprived)","2","3","4","5 (least deprived)", "0 (missing)"))
[...]
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = recode(cov_cat_smoking_status, "N" = "Never smoker",
                                                            "S" = "Current smoker",
                                                            "E" = "Ever smoker",
                                                            "M" = "Missing"))
input$cov_cat_smoking_status <- ordered(input$cov_cat_smoking_status, levels = c("Never smoker","Ever smoker","Current smoker","Missing"))
[...]
input_factor_vars <- input_factor_vars %>% mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.character(cov_cat_smoking_status)) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = replace_na(cov_cat_smoking_status, "Missing")) %>%
    mutate(cov_cat_smoking_status = as.factor(cov_cat_smoking_status))
input_factor_vars$cov_cat_smoking_status <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_smoking_status, ref = "Never smoker")
[...]
input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity <- relevel(input_factor_vars$cov_cat_ethnicity, ref = "White")
result.explanation: The paper states that a missing category is included for ethnicity, smoking status, and IMD, but the code only explicitly creates missing categories for smoking status and IMD; ethnicity is merely releveled to "White" with no added missing level. Therefore, the coding specification does not fully match the paper.

result.code_value:
surv_formula_age_cat_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex", "+ sub_cat_age_group"
)
[...] 
surv_formula_age_categorical <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+ sub_cat_age_group"
)
[...] 
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_age_group = ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=18 & input$cov_num_age<=39, "18_39", input$cov_cat_age_group))
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_age_group = ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=40 & input$cov_num_age<=59, "40_59", input$cov_cat_age_group))
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_age_group = ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=60 & input$cov_num_age<=79, "60_79", input$cov_cat_age_group))
input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_age_group = ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=80, "80_105", input$cov_cat_age_group))
input$cov_cat_age_group <- factor(input$cov_cat_age_group, ordered = TRUE)

result.code_value:
if(analysis == "all_vax_c"){
  input <- read_rds("output/input_stage1_all.rds")
  input <- input %>% dplyr::select(-lcovid_surv, -lcovid_cens) %>%
    dplyr::rename(lcovid_surv = lcovid_surv_vax_c, lcovid_cens = lcovid_cens_vax_c)
}
[...] surv_formula_age_cat_sex <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  "cov_cat_sex", "+ sub_cat_age_group"
)
[...] fit_cox_model_categorical <-rms::cph(formula= as.formula(surv_formula_age_cat_sex),
                                data= input, weight=input$weight,surv = TRUE,x=TRUE,y=TRUE)
[...] input_select <- input_select %>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date_vax_c=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                                 vax_covid_date1,
                                                                                 death_date, 
                                                                                 cohort_end_date,
                                                                                 na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv_vax_c<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date_vax_c - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens_vax_c = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date_vax_c & 
                                                                          out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                          !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...] input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_age_group = ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=18 & input$cov_num_age<=39, "18_39",
                                                       ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=40 & input$cov_num_age<=59,"40_59",
                                                              ifelse(input$cov_num_age>=60 & input$cov_num_age<=79, "60_79",
                                                                     "80_105"))))
input$cov_cat_age_group <- factor(input$cov_cat_age_group, ordered = TRUE)
result.explanation: The paper specifies coded long COVID as the outcome, time from cohort start, a pre‑vaccination cohort censored at first vaccine, and age as categorical (18–39 reference). The code implements Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) with pre‑vaccination censoring, defines the same age groups, and fits a Cox model including sub_cat_age_group.

result.explanation: The code sets the same parameters as described in the paper: post-vaccination follow-up starts 14 days after dose 2, ends at event/death/31 Mar 2022, uses categorical age groups (18–39, 40–59, 60–79, 80–105) in the Cox model, and applies 1:20 sampling with inverse-probability weights and robust standard errors. Therefore, the parameter specifications match.

analysis.brief_description: Cox proportional hazards model in the post-vaccination cohort estimating hazard ratios for age categories (40–59, 60–79, 80–105 vs 18–39 reference) for time to coded long COVID.
analysis.model_specification: “Hazard ratios by age group (40 to 59, 60 to 79 and 80 to 105 years compared with 18 to 39 years(reference)), were estimated from models including age as a categorical variable, instead of a cubic spline.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Age categories: “Where categorised, age groups were: 18-39, 40-59, 60-79, 80-105 years.” Comparative specification: “Hazard ratios by age group (40 to 59, 60 to 79 and 80 to 105 years compared with 18 to 39 years(reference))…” Cohort: “a post-vaccination cohort, with follow-up start date 14 days after receipt of second COVID-19 vaccine dose and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022.”
analysis.transformation_specification: Categorical age in model: “…estimated from models including age as a categorical variable, instead of a cubic spline.” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses – age modeled categorically for this analysis; Results: Demographic factors – Post-vaccination cohort

analysis.brief_description: Cox proportional hazards model in the post-COVID diagnosis cohort estimating the association of COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised) with time to coded long COVID, adjusted for other patient characteristics.
analysis.model_specification: “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Exposure of interest: “we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” Severity definition: “Hospitalisation for COVID-19 was defined as a hospital admission record with confirmed COVID-19 diagnosis in primary position within 28 days of the first COVID-19 diagnosis and COVID-19 without hospitalisation as a COVID-19 diagnosis that was not followed by hospitalisation within 28 days.” Adjustment set context: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” Cohort: “a post-COVID diagnosis cohort… with follow-up start date the first recorded COVID-19 diagnosis …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”
analysis.location: Methods: Statistical analyses – additional factor in post-COVID diagnosis cohort; Results: Health behavioural and clinical factors – Post-COVID diagnosis cohort

result.paper_value: “a post-COVID diagnosis cohort, defined regardless of vaccination status, with follow-up start date the first recorded COVID-19 diagnosis and end date the earliest of first record of any long COVID code, death date, or 31 March 2022” [...] “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” [...] “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” [...] “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” [...] “Hospitalisation for COVID-19 was defined as a hospital admission record with confirmed COVID-19 diagnosis in primary position within 28 days of the first COVID-19 diagnosis and COVID-19 without hospitalisation as a COVID-19 diagnosis that was not followed by hospitalisation within 28 days.”
result.code_value:
if(cohort == "infected"){
  input <- input %>% filter(!is.na(out_covid_date) &out_covid_date>= index_date & out_covid_date <= cohort_end_date)
  input$index_date = input$out_covid_date
}
[...] input_select <- input_select%>% rowwise() %>% mutate(fup_end_date=min(out_first_long_covid_date, 
                                                                        death_date, 
                                                                        cohort_end_date,
                                                                        na.rm = TRUE))
input_select$lcovid_surv<- as.numeric(input_select$fup_end_date - input_select$index_date)+1
input_select <- input_select %>% mutate(lcovid_cens = ifelse((out_first_long_covid_date <= fup_end_date & 
                                                                  out_first_long_covid_date >= index_date &
                                                                  !is.na(out_first_long_covid_date)), 1, 0))
[...] input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- "no_infection"
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),
                                          "non_hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date) & 
                                            !is.na(input$hospital_covid) &
                                            (input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date>=0 &
                                               input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date<29),
                                          "hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
[...] if(cohort == "infected"){
  input <- input %>% mutate(cov_cat_covid_phenotype = sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
}
[...] covariate_names <- names(input)[grep("cov_", names(input))]
covariate_names <- covariate_names[-grep("age", covariate_names)]
surv_formula <- paste0(
  "Surv(lcovid_surv, lcovid_cens) ~ ",
  paste(covariate_names, collapse = "+"),
  "+rms::rcs(cov_num_age,parms=knot_placement)"
)
result.explanation: The code constructs the post-COVID (infected) cohort with index_date set to the first COVID-19 date, defines the outcome as time to coded long COVID, and includes severity (cov_cat_covid_phenotype: hospitalised vs non_hospitalised) among the covariates along with all other patient characteristics. This matches the paper’s specified variables and designations.

analysis.brief_description: Cox proportional hazards model in the post-COVID diagnosis cohort estimating the association of COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised) with time to coded long COVID, adjusted for other patient characteristics.
analysis.model_specification: “In the post-COVID diagnosis cohort, we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.”
analysis.variable_specification: Outcome/time scale: “The outcome was clinically coded long COVID… Time to the outcome event was defined as days from participant specific follow-up start date…” Exposure of interest: “we included COVID-19 severity (hospitalised vs non-hospitalised COVID-19) as an additional factor.” Severity definition: “Hospitalisation for COVID-19 was defined as a hospital admission record with confirmed COVID-19 diagnosis in primary position within 28 days of the first COVID-19 diagnosis and COVID-19 without hospitalisation as a COVID-19 diagnosis that was not followed by hospitalisation within 28 days.” Adjustment set context: “then all patient characteristics were included in a multivariable Cox PH model.” Cohort: “a post-COVID diagnosis cohort… with follow-up start date the first recorded COVID-19 diagnosis …”
analysis.transformation_specification: Age functional form: “Age was modelled using a restricted cubic spline…” Sampling/weights/SEs: “For computational efficiency, we used the full population with coded long COVID and a randomly sampled population without coded long COVID with a ratio of 1:20. We used inverse probability weighting and robust standard errors to account for the sampling approach.” Missing data: “We included a missing category for ethnicity, smoking status and IMD.”

## Hospitalized COVID defined as admitted to hospitals within 28 days following COVID positive test[...]input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- "no_infection"[...]input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),

result.code_value:
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- "no_infection"
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date),
                                        "non_hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- ifelse(!is.na(input$out_covid_date) & 
                                          !is.na(input$hospital_covid) &
                                          (input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date>=0 &
                                             input$hospital_covid-input$out_covid_date<29),
                                        "hospitalised",input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)
input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype <- as.factor(input$sub_cat_covid_phenotype)[...]
if(analysis == "infected"){
    surv_formula <- paste0(surv_formula, " + cov_cat_covid_phenotype")
}
result.explanation: The paper specifies coding COVID-19 severity as hospitalised within 28 days versus not hospitalised. The code implements the same 28-day rule to assign "hospitalised" vs "non_hospitalised" and includes this factor in the infected cohort model.
